title: Quick check: When NOT to build an agent

questions.prompt: You're routing customer messages. Audit shows your 6-tool agent always calls `classify_intent` then `route_to_team` in the same order every time. Right move?

choices.text: Replace with a workflow — actually, a single LLM call returning structured output `{intent, team}`. The agent is doing fixed work; the agent overhead buys nothing. Cost typically drops 90%+, latency drops from seconds to sub-second, and reliability improves because there are fewer moving parts.

questions.explanation: When the steps are predictable, agents add cost and failure modes for no benefit. Single LLM call with structured output is the cheapest, fastest, most reliable pattern for classify-and-route. Save agents for tasks where the path genuinely depends on intermediate results.

questions.prompt: What's the right bootstrap order for AI features?

choices.text: Always start with an agent — promote down only if necessary.

Start with the simplest pattern: (1) single LLM call with the best prompt + structured output you can write; (2) if not enough, a workflow (predetermined steps); (3) if not enough, an agent with step caps and observability; (4) if still not enough, multi-agent. Most teams skip 1–2 and build agents that should have been single calls.
Always use the most capable model.

questions.explanation: Bootstrap upward, not downward. Single calls are cheapest, fastest, most reliable; workflows are next; agents are expensive flexible options for genuinely open-ended problems. Most production AI value is created by good single calls and workflows — agents are a smaller slice than the marketing implies.
